---
title: ""
format:
  html:
    theme: minty
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    page-layout: full
    smooth-scroll: true
    title-block-banner: false
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show Code"
sidebar: false
---

```{=html}
<style>

/* ===== NAVBAR ===== */

.navbar{
  background-color:#0b3d2e !important;
  border-bottom:1px solid #14532d;
}

.navbar-brand,
.navbar a{
  color:white !important;
  font-weight:600;
}

.navbar a:hover{
  color:#bbf7d0 !important;
}

/* ===== GENERAL ===== */

body{
  background: linear-gradient(to bottom right, #eefbf3, #dff5e7);
  font-family:'Segoe UI', sans-serif;
  color:#1f2937;
}

main.content{
  padding-top:12px !important;
}

/* ===== HERO ===== */

.hero{
  background: linear-gradient(135deg,#14532d,#22c55e);
  color:white;
  padding:55px 40px;
  border-radius:22px;
  text-align:center;
  margin-bottom:24px;
  box-shadow:0px 8px 28px rgba(0,0,0,0.12);
}

.hero h1{
  font-size:2.8rem;
  font-weight:800;
  margin-bottom:10px;
}

.hero p{
  max-width:750px;
  margin:auto;
  font-size:1.05rem;
  line-height:1.7;
  opacity:0.96;
}

/* ===== CARDS ===== */

.card-box{
  background:white;
  border-radius:18px;
  padding:26px;
  box-shadow:0px 4px 16px rgba(0,0,0,0.06);
  border:1px solid #dcfce7;
  margin-bottom:18px;
}

.card-box h2{
  color:#166534;
  margin-bottom:12px;
}

/* ===== QUESTIONS ===== */

.qa-box{
  background:#f8fffa;
  border:1px solid #dcfce7;
  padding:18px;
  border-radius:14px;
  margin-bottom:12px;
}

/* ===== FOOTER ===== */

.footer{
  text-align:center;
  margin-top:28px;
  padding:12px;
  color:#166534;
  font-size:0.95rem;
}

</style>

::: {.hero}

Assignment 1 – Task 3

Video Summary & Data Analysis

Industrial Engineering and Data Science integration based on Cem Vardar’s interview.

:::

::: {.card-box}

Summary: Industrial Engineering and Data Science

I watched the interview with Cem Vardar, who holds a PhD from Arizona State University and has worked at companies such as Intel and Carvana.

The main idea of the interview is the strong relationship between Industrial Engineering (IE) and Data Science in real-world applications.

He explains that Industrial Engineering focuses on solving problems in complex systems, while Data Science should be seen as a tool for solving business problems, rather than a separate career path.

A key concept he introduces is “Evolutionary Design”, which emphasizes improving systems gradually through small, data-driven changes instead of completely replacing them at once.

He also highlights that foundational tools like SQL and Excel remain essential, even as technology evolves rapidly.

:::

::: {.card-box}

Questions

::: {.qa-box}

1. Open-Ended Question

What is Cem Vardar's "Evolutionary Design" approach, and why does he suggest it instead of replacing an entire system?

Answer:
It refers to improving systems step-by-step using small, incremental changes. This approach is preferred because full system replacements are risky, complex, and often fail in real business environments, while gradual improvements are more reliable.

:::

::: {.qa-box}

2. Multiple-Choice Question

Which tool is described as a “must-have” skill for over 20 years?

a) Neural Networks
b) Deep Fakes
c) SQL (Structured Query Language)
d) ChatGPT

Answer: c) SQL

:::

:::

::: {.card-box}

AI Disclosure

I used Gemini 3 Flash to:

Summarize the interview transcript
Format the questions
Assist with clarity improvements in the write-up

:::

::: {.card-box}

Video Link

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fi8-phj1X1A

:::

::: {.card-box}

Data Analysis Section

In this section, the polls_us_election_2016 dataset from the dslabs package is analyzed using R.

library(dslabs)
library(dplyr)

data("polls_us_election_2016")
data <- polls_us_election_2016

my_first <- "Bora"
my_birth_year <- 2004

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
k

if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(data, k)
} else {
  tail(data, k)
}

sum(is.na(data))

na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(data)), decreasing = TRUE)
head(na_per_column, 8)

new_data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), my_birth_year + k, .)),
    across(where(is.character), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), paste0(my_first, "_", k), .)),
    across(where(is.factor), ~ {
      x <- as.character(.)
      x[is.na(x)] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
      x
    })
  )

if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  head(new_data, k)
} else {
  tail(new_data, k)
}

sum(is.na(new_data))
anyNA(new_data)

I also used Gemini AI to debug and correct errors in my R code.

:::

<div class="footer">

Made with 💚 using R and Quarto

</div> ```
